title: Buying in bulk to reduce packaging waste
subtitle: When bulk buying actually reduces packaging and when it just moves waste elsewhere
excerpt: Buying in larger quantities reduces the packaging-to-product ratio. A 5 kg bag of flour uses less plastic per kilogram than five 1 kg bags. But bulk buying only reduces packaging waste if the product is used before it spoils. Buying 5 kg of something that gets thrown away half-used generates more waste overall than buying in smaller portions.
sourceNotes: WRAP household food and drink waste study (2021). Ellen MacArthur Foundation packaging waste data (2022). Defra retail packaging statistics. Zero Waste Home (Johnson, Bea, 2013).

content.text: The packaging-to-product ratio improves with scale. A 500 g pot of oats has roughly 20 g of plastic per kg of product. A 2 kg bag has around 8 g per kg. A 5 kg bag drops to 4 g per kg. The reduction is real. But it is only a saving if you use all 5 kg before the product degrades, and if storing a large quantity does not require a secondary container that adds its own material footprint.
Products that work well for bulk buying

Dry staples with long shelf life: flour, oats, dried pasta, rice, lentils, dried beans. These keep for 12 to 24 months in a sealed container. Buying the largest size you can use within one year always reduces the packaging ratio.

Cleaning and laundry products: concentrated detergents and cleaning liquids have a high packaging-to-volume ratio when bought in small formats. A 5 litre container of washing-up liquid, used by decanting into a smaller pump dispenser, uses less plastic than ten 500 ml bottles.
Cooking oil, vinegar, soy sauce: all have good shelf stability and benefit from buying in the largest format your storage allows.
Products where bulk buying often fails
Fresh produce: a 2 kg bag of carrots or onions where half go soft before being used generates food waste that outweighs the packaging saving.
Products you use rarely: a large format of something used once a month generates its own waste if it passes its best-before date.
Items with complex packaging: some large formats use more plastic per unit because of inner trays and dividers.

Refill shops (where you bring your own container and buy loose products by weight) offer packaging reduction close to 100 percent for the products they carry. They are the strongest option where available. Their product range is limited compared to a supermarket, so bulk buying from supermarkets fills the gaps. The combination works well: loose pulses, cereals, and cleaning products from a refill shop; large-format dry goods, oil, and cleaning concentrates from a supermarket.

Bulk staples need airtight storage or they attract pests and absorb moisture. Use existing large glass jars, metal tins, or food-grade plastic containers. Reuse what you have before buying new storage containers: buying a set of matching canisters specifically for bulk buying adds material cost that offsets the packaging saving for the first several months of use.
